footer.mission: Oravan is free, nonpartisan civic infrastructure. No accounts, no tracking: your data never leaves your device.
footer.aiNote: Bill summaries and scripts are AI-drafted and clearly marked. Always review before you use them.
footer.lore: Oravan is a coined name — part oration, part caravan: one voice setting out, many arriving together.

home.heroTitle: Your voice matters. Make it heard.
home.heroSub: Five minutes is all it takes. Find your representatives, understand the bills that touch your life in plain language, and say your piece. No account. No app. Just you and the phone.
home.zipHelp: Stays on your device. We never see or store it.
home.zipInvalid: That doesn't look like a US ZIP code. Try 5 digits.
home.topSub: The bills moving right now, decoded into plain language by AI.
home.heroJump: Or jump straight to this week's callable bills
home.walkthroughSub: What actually happens, step by step — from plain language to a 30-second call.
home.how1Body: Your ZIP code finds your House representative and two senators, with their DC and local office numbers.
home.how2Title: Pick a bill, get the plain version
home.how2Body: Every active bill is decoded from legal language into words a neighbor would use.
home.how3Body: Tell us where you stand and get a 30-second script. Edit it until it sounds like you.
home.how4Body: Offices tally voicemails the same as live calls. After hours works too. Then log how it went.
home.whyBody: Yes, and better than email, petitions, or posts. Congressional staff tally calls daily and report the counts to the member.
home.privacyBody: No sign-up, no cookies that follow you, no political profile stored on any server. Everything personal lives in your browser, and you can erase it with one tap.

reps.title: Your representatives
reps.sub: Every US resident has one House representative and two senators. Here are yours, with every number that reaches them.
reps.noZip: Enter your ZIP code to find your three members of Congress.
reps.multiDistrict: Your ZIP code spans more than one congressional district. We've shown both below — your street address decides which one is yours. Not sure? Your senators are the same either way.
reps.zipNotFound: We couldn't match that ZIP code to a congressional district. Double-check it, or look up your member directly at house.gov.
reps.delegateNote: {state} elects a delegate to the House instead of a voting representative, and has no U.S. senators. Delegates can speak in committees, introduce bills, and advocate for you. Your call still counts.
reps.vacantSeatBody: No one currently holds this House seat. This page will update once a successor is sworn in.
reps.refineTitle: Optional: narrow it to your exact district
reps.refinePrivacy: Your street address is used once to find your district, sent nowhere else, and never stored.
reps.addressInvalid: That looks too short to be a street address. Try something like 421 8th Ave.
reps.addressNotFound: We couldn't find that address. Check the street number and spelling, or keep using the full list below.
reps.refineUnavailable: We couldn't check your address right now. Every possible district is still shown below, so nothing is missing. Try again later if you'd like.
reps.refineRateLimited: You've checked several addresses in a short time. Take a breath and try again soon.
reps.refinedNote: This is your district, found from the street address you entered. The address was used once and never stored. Your senators are the same for the whole state.
reps.refinedOutsideZip: Your address falls in a district our ZIP map didn't expect for {zip}. A street address is more precise than a ZIP map, so this is the one to trust.
reps.nextTitle: Now you know who to call
reps.nextSub: Here's what's worth a call to them this week.

bills.title: Active bills, decoded
bills.sub: Every bill moving through Congress right now, translated into plain language by AI. Filter by what matters to you.
bills.searchPlaceholder: Search by topic, keyword, or bill number…
bills.noResults: No bills match. Try clearing a filter.

bandSub.now: The most active bills in Congress right now. A call lands hardest here.
bandSub.moving: Advancing through committees and onto the calendar.
bandSub.radar: Earlier stages: worth watching, less time pressure.

bills.decodedPending: We're still translating this one into plain language.
bills.interestsNote: Topic picks are saved only on this device, never on a server.

bill.aiDisclaimer: AI-drafted summary. Verify it against the official text before you act on it.
bill.viewOfficial: Read the official bill on Congress.gov
bill.actSub: Three steps: where you stand, your script, the call.
bill.concernNote: Your concern gets logged right alongside the yes and no calls. Staffers usually won't debate policy on the spot — and they don't need to for your call to count.
bill.scriptHint: Edit anything. It works best when it sounds like you, and the [brackets] are yours to fill in.
bill.scriptDisclaimer: AI-drafted. Review and edit before you call.
bill.scriptError: Couldn't draft a script right now. Try again in a minute.
bill.rateLimited: You've requested several scripts in a short time. Take a breath and try again soon.
bill.needZip: Enter your ZIP code to see your representatives' numbers.
bill.callWho: Call any of your three, or all of them. One call per office is plenty.
bill.afterHoursBody: Voicemails are tallied exactly like live calls. Calling evenings or weekends and leaving a message counts just as much. Mention your ZIP code so they know you're a constituent.
bill.officeHoursOpenBody: Congressional offices generally keep weekday business hours, roughly 9am–5:30pm Eastern — it's inside that window right now, so a live staffer may answer. If you land on voicemail anyway, it's tallied exactly the same.
bill.officeHoursClosedBody: It's outside typical business hours right now (weekdays, roughly 9am–5:30pm Eastern) — voicemail is the likely outcome, and that's a fine place to start: leave your name and ZIP, and it counts exactly like a live call.
bill.staffNote: A staffer (often an intern) answers, tallies your position, and moves on. No debate, no quiz. Under a minute.
bill.outcomeLogged: Logged, and saved only on this device. Thank you for calling.
bill.hearFirstBody: Usually one or two rings, then a staffer says the office name. Or a short menu, or voicemail. All three are fine: say your script, or leave it as the message.
bill.outcomeChange: Tap another option to change it.
bill.repsError: We couldn't load your representatives just now.
bill.whyLink: First call ever? Two minutes on why it works
bill.callWhoOne: Your delegate is your voice in the House. One call to their office counts.

sec.cost: What does it cost, and who pays?

journey.nowCommittee: a {chamber, select, House {House} Senate {Senate} other {House}} committee is reviewing it.
journey.nowFloor: it's headed for a {chamber, select, House {House} Senate {Senate} other {House}} floor vote.
journey.nowPassed: it passed the {chamber, select, House {House} Senate {Senate} other {House}} and now goes to the {other, select, House {House} Senate {Senate} other {Senate}}.
journey.nowConference: both chambers are reconciling their versions.
journey.nowSigned: the President signed it. It's law.
journey.nowVetoed: the President vetoed it. Congress can override with two-thirds of both chambers.
journey.backTrailer: If the {other, select, House {House} Senate {Senate} other {Senate}} changes it, it goes back to the {chamber, select, House {House} Senate {Senate} other {House}} before reaching the President.
journey.nowIntroduced: it was just introduced and is headed to committee.

bill.loggedFirst: That was your first call. Most people never make one. You just did.
bill.loggedFifth: Five calls logged. The offices are starting to know your ZIP code.
bill.loggedTenth: Ten calls. What started as nerves is now a habit.
bill.generating1: Reading the bill so you don't have to…
bill.firstCallBody: Talking to a congressional staffer can feel intimidating the first time. Calling after hours is the gentlest way to start: no live conversation, just a voicemail that counts exactly the same. Tonight works as well as right now.
bill.preDialBody: Staffers field calls like this all day, and it's not a debate — about thirty seconds and you're done. A live pickup or a voicemail count exactly the same, so either one is a good outcome.

walkthrough.label: How a call works — a five-step demo
walkthrough.sampleNote: Shown with a real bill: H.R. 1787, the Roberto Clemente Commemorative Coin Act.

decode.title: A bill, decoded
decode.body: Oravan turns every bill into plain language — always labeled as AI-drafted, with the official text one tap away.

stance.title: Say where you stand
stance.body: Support, oppose, or concerned — all three count the same. The demo taps one to keep the tour moving; the choice is always yours.

script.title: Your 30-second script
script.body: A ready-to-read script appears. It's AI-drafted and labeled as such — review and edit it until it sounds like you before you call.

call.title: Make the call
call.body: Dial any of your three members' offices. A friendly staffer answers, tallies your position, and that's it — about 30 seconds.

logged.title: Logged — on this device only
logged.body: Mark how it went. Your call history lives in your browser, never on a server. That's the whole loop.

phone.billHeadline: U.S. Mint would strike Roberto Clemente coins in 2027
phone.billTldr: Three commemorative coins in 2027 — surcharges go to his foundation, at no cost to taxpayers.
phone.scriptSnippet: Hi, my name is [YOUR NAME], and I'm a constituent from [YOUR TOWN OR ZIP]. I'm calling about H.R. 1787, the Roberto Clemente Commemorative Coin Act…

coverage.heading: How it's being covered
coverage.subhead: Real reporting on this bill, labeled by each outlet's political lean.
coverage.leanNote: Lean labels describe the news outlet, not this bill or any party. Ratings by AllSides.
coverage.oneSidedNote: Coverage of this bill so far comes from one side of the spectrum. Read it with that in mind.

news.heading: In the news
news.subhead: Bills drawing real coverage across the press right now.

why.title: Why calling works
why.intro: It feels too small to matter. It isn't. Here's what actually happens when you call a congressional office, and why one phone call outweighs a hundred posts.
why.tallyBody: Congressional offices keep a running count of constituent positions on active issues. Those tallies reach the member's desk while a vote is still undecided. Reporting has documented offices overwhelmed by sustained call volume, and members shifting position because of it.
why.emailBody: Congress receives tens of millions of emails a year. Form letters and petition signatures are heavily discounted, and AI-generated mail floods even more so. A phone call is scarce, verifiable effort from a real constituent — that's exactly why it carries more weight.
why.voicemailBody: Offices check voicemail every day and tally messages identically to live calls. If talking to a person isn't for you, after-hours voicemail is a fully effective alternative, not a consolation prize.
why.scriptBody: Your name, your ZIP or town (so they know you're a constituent), the bill, your position, one sentence why. That's it. The whole call takes under a minute, and the staffer is just writing down your position. There's no debate.
why.respectBody: The person answering is usually a young staffer having a long day. Being brief and kind doesn't dilute your message. It makes it land.
why.cta: Find a bill worth calling about

impact.title: Your impact
impact.sub: Stored only in this browser. Oravan has no servers that know who you are.
impact.emptyBody: When you log a call from a bill page, your history shows up here, visible to you alone.
impact.emptyCta: Find a bill to call about
impact.eraseBody: Everything Oravan knows about you lives in this browser. Erase it any time.
impact.eraseConfirm: Erase your ZIP, interests, and call history from this device? This can't be undone.

privacy.title: Privacy, in plain language
privacy.p1: Oravan has no user accounts. We never ask for your name, email, address, or anything that identifies you.
privacy.p2: Your ZIP code, your issue interests, and your call history are stored only in your browser's local storage, on your device. They are never transmitted to, or stored on, our servers. The Impact page has a one-tap button that erases all of it.
privacy.p3: When you ask for a call script, your chosen stance and the bill are sent to our server to draft the text, processed in memory, and not linked to any identity. We rate-limit by network address to prevent abuse; we don't keep a log tying addresses to political positions.
privacy.p4: No analytics trackers, no advertising pixels, no cookies used to identify you. The only cookie-like storage is your language preference.
privacy.p5: This matters because political beliefs are sensitive data. The safest database is the one that doesn't exist, so we didn't build one.
privacy.contact: Questions? Oravan is built in the open — every line of code is inspectable.
privacy.p7: One optional exception to "we never ask": if your ZIP code covers more than one congressional district, you can type your street address to pin down yours. It goes to our server once, is passed straight to the U.S. Census Bureau's public geocoder (so your device never contacts census.gov directly), and is never stored or written to any log. Only the district number comes back.

about.title: About Oravan
about.intro: Oravan is free, nonpartisan civic infrastructure: find your federal representatives, understand active bills in plain language, and make a 30-second call — in English or Spanish, with no account and no server-side record of your political activity.
about.fundingBody: Oravan runs without ads, without partisan grants, and without selling data — there's no data to sell. A donation option, hosted entirely off Oravan's own servers, is on the way.
about.supportBody: Oravan is free, ad-free, and takes no partisan money. Your gift helps us: track every bill moving through Congress each week, keep the plain-language decodes current and human-reviewed, and keep call scripts free in English and Spanish. We store no donor political activity, ever — gifts are tax-deductible and processed off Oravan's own servers by our fiscal sponsor, The Hack Foundation, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit.

citations.title: Cite Oravan
citations.intro: Oravan is built to be a source you can cite and correct: every bill has one permanent address, a dated freshness stamp, and a clear line between the official record and AI-drafted plain language. This page spells out exactly what that promise means — for reporters, librarians, and anyone building on Oravan's data or MCP server — and explains how to report an error.
citations.urlBody: Every bill gets one permanent address in each language, built only from its own bill type, number, and Congress — never from its status. A citation written the day a bill is introduced still resolves after it's signed, fails, or expires; the address is never reused or reassigned.
citations.urlExampleLabel: Example — H.R. 1787, the same bill used in Oravan's own demo material:
citations.asOfBody: Every page states the date of Oravan's last successful nightly sync with Congress.gov — not a live feed, and not the date you happen to load the page. Cite that date, not today's date, as the moment the underlying facts were last checked. It's the same value as the {asOfField} field in every MCP tool response.
citations.sourceBody: Bill status, dates, sponsor, and text come from Congress.gov. Member and district-office data comes from the public-domain unitedstates/congress-legislators project. Both refresh only through Oravan's own nightly sync. The MCP citation envelope states the source in whichever language a query requested — both language versions are shown below, since this page is read by both:
citations.aiBody: Two kinds of text appear on a bill page, and they're never presented the same way. The official record — title, status, sponsor, dates, last action, the Congress.gov link — is copied, not generated. The plain-language layer — the headline, the summary, and the what/who/why/cost sections — is AI-drafted, and every one of those fields is reviewed by a person before it publishes; a new bill enters Oravan's corpus only once that review is done, in both languages. Wherever AI-drafted content appears — the site, the MCP server, an embed — it carries this label, in the language the request used; both language versions are shown below:
citations.aiCallScript: Call scripts work under a stricter rule still: they're drafted only inside Oravan's own site, and a caller must read and can edit the script before placing a call. Oravan's MCP server never generates one — it hands back a link to the on-site flow instead, so that human-review step can't be bypassed by an agent.
citations.licenseOfficialLabel: The underlying official-record data — U.S. public domain, in both languages:
citations.licenseAiLabel: Oravan's own AI-drafted plain-language content, in both languages:
citations.licenseCoverage: AllSides' outlet-lean ratings, shown in each bill's "Read" section, are used under a separate CC BY-NC license and are excluded from the MCP server and from anything commercial — a website-only feature until that's resolved.
citations.correctionBody: Found something wrong — a mistranslation, a misread status, a plain-language summary that misstates what a bill does? Use the feedback button in the footer of this (or any) page and choose "Something's not working." Name the bill and describe the error — that's the same intake Oravan uses for every other bug report, not a separate correction form.
citations.whenConfirmedTitle: What happens when a correction is confirmed
citations.whenConfirmedBody: A confirmed error is fixed at the source — the same reviewed data both languages' decodes are built from. From there, every surface reading that data updates together on Oravan's next deploy: the bill page, its statically generated share-card image, and the MCP server's tool responses all read the identical file, so there's no separate cache to invalidate. The one thing that doesn't move on its own is the freshness stamp — the site-wide "as of" date and the MCP envelope's {asOfField} field both track Oravan's last successful nightly sync, so they advance at the next scheduled sync run after the fix ships, the same way they do every night. An embeddable widget that carries bill text is still in development, not yet shipped; once it exists, it will read this same corrected data with no separate correction step of its own.
citations.backlogNote: A correction that points to a broader pattern — not a one-off slip — gets tracked for Oravan's ongoing Spanish-language review process rather than patched quietly and forgotten.

notFound.title: Page not found
notFound.body: That page doesn't exist, but your representatives do.

terms.title: Terms, in plain language
terms.p1: Oravan is a free, nonpartisan civic tool. It helps you find your members of Congress, understand active bills, and call about them. It is not affiliated with Congress, any government agency, party, or campaign.
terms.p2: Bill summaries, headlines, and call scripts are drafted by AI and are clearly labeled. They can contain mistakes. The official bill text on Congress.gov is always the authority, and you are responsible for reviewing and editing anything before you say it.
terms.p3: Oravan comes with no guarantees. We do our best to keep representative data and bill statuses current, but offices change numbers and bills move daily — verify anything that matters.
terms.p4: What you do with the phone is yours: you place the calls, you choose the words. Be honest, be brief, and be kind to the staffer who answers.
terms.p5: We have no accounts and store nothing about you on our servers; everything personal stays in your browser. The privacy page explains this in full.

feedback.title: Beta feedback
feedback.intro: Oravan is in beta. Tell us what's broken, confusing, or missing.
feedback.notice: Don't include personal details. Feedback is stored as text in a private tracker; we can't reply.
feedback.success: Got it — thank you. Every note during the beta shapes what gets fixed first.
feedback.errorIncomplete: Pick a category and write a message first.
feedback.errorRateLimited: You've sent several notes in a short time. Take a breath and try again soon.
feedback.errorGeneric: That didn't go through. Try again in a minute.

freshness.quietWeekBody: No bill has cleared the bar for an urgent call right now — a higher bar than making the news. Check back soon, or browse everything moving through Congress.
freshness.dataStaleBody: We can't confirm what's most urgent right now — our last successful data check was {date}. This isn't a quiet week, it's a stale one.
freshness.staleNote: Our last successful data check was {date} — newer activity in Congress may not be shown yet.

embed.multiDistrictTitle: This ZIP spans more than one district
embed.multiDistrictBody: This embedded widget can't take a street address — that stays on the main Oravan site. Both candidate districts are shown below; your senators are the same either way.
embed.multiDistrictBodyBrandless: This embedded widget can't take a street address — that step happens on the full site. Both candidate districts are shown below; your senators are the same either way.
embed.openFullLookup: Refine with your address on Oravan
embed.billNotFound: We couldn't find that bill in our data.

manifest.description: Find your reps. Understand the bills. Make the call.

embeds.title: Embed Oravan on your site
embeds.intro: Free, self-serve widgets — no sales call, no account, no server-side data about your visitors. Pick a widget, set a few options, and copy one script tag.
embeds.widgetRepLookupHint: Visitors enter a ZIP code and get their three federal representatives.
embeds.widgetBillCardHint: One bill's plain-language headline, status, and freshness date.
embeds.localeNote: Visitors can always switch languages inside the widget — the toggle can never be removed.
embeds.billSearchPlaceholder: Search by bill number, topic, or title…
embeds.previewPending: Choose a bill above to preview the widget.
embeds.snippetHint: Paste it anywhere in your page's HTML, wherever you want the widget to appear.
embeds.snippetPending: Choose a bill above to generate a working snippet.
embeds.docsIsolationTitle: An iframe, not a script running on your page
embeds.docsIsolationBody: The widget loads inside a cross-origin iframe, the same browser mechanism that keeps a video embed or a payment form isolated from the page around it. That isn't a policy promise — it's the browser enforcing it. Your page's own scripts, analytics, and tag managers cannot read what a visitor types into the widget or observe anything happening inside it, because they run in a different origin's sandbox.
embeds.docsPrivacyTitle: What the widget collects about your visitors: nothing
embeds.docsPrivacyCookies: Zero cookies. No response from the embed ever sets one.
embeds.docsPrivacyThirdParty: Zero third-party requests. Every asset the widget loads — fonts, styles, data — comes from Oravan's own origin. Nothing calls out to an ad network, an analytics vendor, or any other third party.
embeds.docsPrivacyZip: ZIP code only. The representative-lookup widget never asks for a street address inside the iframe; a visitor who needs the more precise, split-district lookup is linked out to the main Oravan site for that step.
embeds.docsPrivacyNoData: No visitor data reaches Oravan beyond an ordinary page load. A ZIP code a visitor enters is used once, in memory, to answer that one lookup, and is never logged or stored.
embeds.docsPrivacyTested: These aren't claims made only in this copy — they're enforced by automated tests in Oravan's own public repository, on every change, before it ships.
embeds.docsThemingBody: Theming works the same on both widgets and is limited to three CSS custom properties, each validated on Oravan's server before it can reach a style: an accent color (a hex code), a corner-radius style (sharp, soft, or round), and a font (system or serif). There's no way to inject arbitrary CSS or JavaScript into the widget — that's a deliberate security boundary, not a missing feature.
embeds.docsAttributionBody: Every widget shows a small "Powered by Oravan" link, pointing back to the canonical page on oravan.org. The brandless option removes the Oravan name from the widget chrome (its accessible titles and fallback labels) but keeps that attribution link — it's how the free tier stays free. Removing attribution itself (data-attribution="none") is reserved for licensed partners: there's no technical lock yet, so we say it plainly — if you want it, write us for a license. The AI-content label is never removable, on any tier.
embeds.docsLoaderBody: The script tag your snippet includes is a single, dependency-free file under 5KB. All it does is create the iframe, size it to fit its content, and relay that size back — nothing is read from your page, and nothing is sent anywhere except the one request that loads the widget itself.
embeds.whiteLabelNote: Removes the Oravan name from widget titles and fallback labels. The "Powered by Oravan" link stays on — removing it is available to licensed partners only; see the attribution note below.

partners.title: Partners
partners.intro: Oravan's widgets are built to live inside other people's pages — newsroom articles, library guides, an organization's action center. Everything on this page is free to use today; here's what each kind of partner gets, and where the licensed tier begins.
partners.newsroomsBody: Drop a representative lookup or a plain-language bill card into any article — two script tags, no account, no tracking of your readers, ever. Spanish isn't a translation layer here: every surface ships in English and Spanish as equals, so an es-first outlet embeds an es-first widget. Theme it to your section colors, or use brandless chrome so the widget reads as part of your page.
partners.librariesBody: Oravan is safe to put in front of every patron: strictly nonpartisan by construction, no accounts, nothing stored server-side, plain language reviewed by people. Link it from a resource guide or embed the lookup on your programs page — it works the same on a library terminal as on a phone.
partners.orgsBody: Nonprofits, unions, and civic groups use the same free widgets as everyone else — themed, in either language, with attribution. A licensed tier for organizations that need attribution-free white-label embeds is coming; the capability exists today and is documented on the embeds page.
partners.licensingBody: Pricing isn't published yet. If your organization wants white-label embeds, attribution removal, or a data partnership, write to us through the feedback form and mention partners — a person reads every note during the beta.
